title: [CRITICAL] conformance: cluster/spatial/signal/stats/integrate case structs declare mode as String AND never read it — fixture mode='Hardened' executes identically to 'Strict' across 5 entire families
description:
Review of crates/fsci-conformance/src/lib.rs on 2026-04-22:

Five case structs declare mode as a plain String rather than the typed
RuntimeMode enum:

    ClusterCase     (line 2858)  pub mode: String
    SpatialCase     (line 3292)  pub mode: String
    SignalCase      (line 3993)  pub mode: String
    StatsCase       (line 4449)  pub mode: String
    IntegrateCase   (line 4979)  pub mode: String

Grep for 'case.mode' reads inside each execute_<family>_case function
body (line numbers 2900..5100): ZERO hits. The mode field is
deserialized from the fixture JSON, stored in the case struct, and NEVER
READ by the Rust execute path. Every function call happens with whatever
default RuntimeMode the fsci-* crate happens to pick internally.

Comparison with the other families that DO use RuntimeMode:

    ValidateTolCase (line 220)   pub mode: RuntimeMode  -- passed to validate_tol_with_audit
    LinalgCase      (line 823)   pub mode: RuntimeMode  -- passed via SolveOptions/InvOptions
    ArrayApiCase    (line 915)   pub mode: RuntimeMode  -- mapped to ExecutionMode at 8824
    SpecialCase     (line 1007)  pub mode: RuntimeMode  -- threaded through special_*
    FftCase         (line 1059)  pub mode: RuntimeMode  -- via FftOptions::with_mode

Impact:

1. frankenscipy-xo4o (2.9% Hardened coverage) was OPTIMISTIC. Of the 17
   total Hardened cases, approximately 5 live in the String-mode
   families (2 spatial + 1 integrate counting exactly; the rest in
   RuntimeMode-mode families). Those 5 Hardened cases execute with
   identical code paths to their Strict counterparts -- no rejection,
   no audit ledger emission, nothing.

2. Hardened fail-closed contract is entirely UNVERIFIED for cluster,
   spatial, signal, stats, integrate. A refactor that breaks Hardened
   rejection in fsci-stats (say, admits NaN through fsci_stats::pearsonr)
   would pass every conformance case. Combined with mhuk (audit ledger
   not emitted for 9 families), Hardened has NO conformance contract
   for these 5 families.

3. Typos silently deserialize. 'mode: \"Hardend\"' (missing e) or
   'mode: \"hardened\"' (lowercase) is a valid String value, bypassing
   any typed enum parse. A fixture that meant to test Hardened behavior
   and typo'd the mode becomes a decorative Strict test.

4. The mode: String fields in the case structs suggest someone intended
   to support richer mode values than the two-variant RuntimeMode enum.
   But no richer mode exists, so the String type is just weaker typing.

Fix:
1. Change mode: String -> mode: RuntimeMode in the 5 case structs.
   Matches the typed approach used elsewhere.
2. Update fsci-cluster, fsci-spatial, fsci-signal, fsci-stats,
   fsci-integrate to accept a RuntimeMode parameter if they don't
   already, and wire case.mode through to every function call in their
   execute_<family>_case.
3. Add a test: for a Hardened case in each of the 5 families, assert
   that fsci_* produces a different result OR emits an audit-ledger
   entry OR returns an error that Strict mode would accept.

Related: xo4o (Hardened coverage, now more alarming), mhuk (audit
ledger emission gap), ivg5 (differential-is-not-differential), p3be
(mode-related exception handling).

title: [HIGH] conformance: stochastic fsci-opt methods (differential_evolution, basinhopping, dual_annealing, brute) and fsci-cluster methods (kmeans, dbscan, mean_shift) have ZERO fixture coverage; fsci-opt 'seed' field has no fixture encoding
description:
Review of crates/fsci-conformance/fixtures/ on 2026-04-22:

Stochastic methods in fsci-* that have ZERO conformance fixture coverage:

fsci-opt (crates/fsci-opt/src/lib.rs):
  1222  differential_evolution   -- global optimization via population
  1486  basinhopping             -- stochastic global minimization
  1620  dual_annealing           -- simulated annealing
  1759  brute                    -- random grid search
  FSCI-P2C-003 fixture uses: Bfgs x9, ConjugateGradient x4, Powell x5,
  TrustExact x2, Brentq x4, Brenth x3, Bisect x5, Ridder x3, Toms748 x4.
  NO stochastic methods. The rand 0.10 bump (commit 6b88d40 chore: rand 0.10)
  changed the stream for these methods; the change is unverified.

fsci-cluster (crates/fsci-cluster/src/lib.rs:150-180 random init):
  kmeans, kmeans2, dbscan, mean_shift, mini_batch_kmeans
  FSCI-P2C-009 uses: linkage x3, fcluster x2, vq x2, whiten x1, cophenet x1,
  inconsistent x1, silhouette x1, adjusted_rand x2, is_valid x3,
  is_monotonic x2, leaves_list x1, num_obs x1.
  NO kmeans/dbscan/mean_shift cases. Stochastic clusterers untested.

fsci-opt seed handling (lib.rs:1291-1293):
    let mut rng: rand::rngs::StdRng = match opts.seed {
        Some(s) => rand::rngs::StdRng::seed_from_u64(s),
        None    => rand::rngs::StdRng::from_rng(&mut rand::rng()),
    };
  When seed is None, differential_evolution uses THREAD-LOCAL
  randomness -- non-deterministic. 0 of 39 optimize fixtures set
  seed; they happen to avoid stochastic methods, but the fixture
  schema (OptimizeCase) doesn't even have a seed field for stochastic
  methods to use.

Impact:
1. fsci-opt's flagship stochastic methods (probably the most-used by
   actual SciPy users for non-convex problems) have ZERO test coverage.
2. Any regression in differential_evolution/basinhopping (including
   the rand 0.10 stream change) is invisible to conformance.
3. fsci-cluster's kmeans and dbscan -- the two most-used clustering
   algorithms -- have ZERO coverage.
4. Even if cases are added, without a seed field in the case struct,
   they'd be non-deterministic and flaky.

Ask:
1. Add seed: Option<u64> to OptimizeCase, ClusterCase. Default to a
   fixture-specified seed; if None, runner rejects the case (fail-closed
   for stochastic methods).
2. Add 10+ stochastic-optimizer cases to FSCI-P2C-003: differential_
   evolution on Rosenbrock, Ackley, Rastrigin with explicit seed=42;
   basinhopping on Himmelblau; dual_annealing on a constrained problem.
3. Add 5+ kmeans/dbscan/mean_shift cases to FSCI-P2C-009 with explicit
   seed values.

title: EPIC: Strict/Hardened Mode Audit Ledger
description:
## Background

SPEC §4-5 define strict vs hardened operating modes. SPEC §16 Security Threat Matrix mandates: metadata incident ledger, stability decision ledger, backend policy ledger, tolerance audit report, compatibility drift report, override audit record. Currently RuntimeMode enum is threaded through SOME validation paths but no centralized audit-ledger emission exists.

## Goal

Implement a structured audit ledger that records: (1) every strict/hardened mode decision, (2) every bounded recovery/repair in hardened mode, (3) every fail-closed rejection, (4) every policy override. Emit as JSON-lines file alongside other conformance artifacts.

## Success Criteria

1. AuditLedger type with append-only recording
2. Every hardened-mode recovery logged with input fingerprint, recovery action, and outcome
3. Every fail-closed rejection logged with rejection reason
4. Ledger emitted as .jsonl artifact in conformance workflow
5. Tests proving ledger captures expected events for known inputs

## Serves Project Goal

SPEC §16 requires "deterministic audit logs for recoveries and policy overrides." Without this, the security model is aspirational.
close_reason: Completed: AuditLedger exists in fsci-runtime, linalg/integrate audit-aware paths record fail_closed and bounded_recovery events, and conformance differential runs emit audit_ledger.jsonl artifacts with tests covering expected events

title: Implement AuditLedger type with append-only recording
description:
## What

Create an AuditLedger struct in fsci-runtime that records structured audit events. Events: ModeDecision (strict/hardened choice), BoundedRecovery (hardened-mode repair action), FailClosed (rejection), PolicyOverride (manual override). Each event: timestamp, input fingerprint (blake3), action taken, outcome.

## Implementation Notes

- Append-only Vec<AuditEvent> or write-through to JSON-lines file
- Serializable via serde for artifact emission
- Thread-safe (Arc<Mutex<...>> or channel-based)
- AuditEvent enum with common fields + event-specific data

## Tests

- Record 3 events → serialize → deserialize → 3 events preserved
- FailClosed event includes rejection reason
- BoundedRecovery event includes recovery action and outcome

## Dependencies

- Requires: AuditLedger type implemented
- Ideally after: CASP wired into linalg (frankenscipy-04wk)

## Tests

- solve() in hardened mode on near-singular input → AuditLedger contains BoundedRecovery event
- solve() in strict mode on invalid input → AuditLedger contains FailClosed event

title: [special] Spherical Bessel, spherical harmonics, associated Legendre
description:
## Goal
Implement spherical special functions used in physics (electromagnetics, quantum mechanics, acoustics, gravitational fields).

## What to implement
- `spherical_jn(n, z)`: spherical Bessel function of first kind j_n(z) = √(π/(2z)) J_{n+1/2}(z)
- `spherical_yn(n, z)`: spherical Bessel of second kind y_n(z) = √(π/(2z)) Y_{n+1/2}(z)
- `spherical_in(n, z)`: modified spherical Bessel i_n(z)
- `spherical_kn(n, z)`: modified spherical Bessel k_n(z)
- `lpmv(m, v, x)`: associated Legendre function P_v^m(x) for integer m, real v
- `sph_harm_y(l, m, theta, phi)`: real spherical harmonic Y_l^m(θ,φ)

## Why this matters
Spherical Bessel functions appear in wave scattering, antenna theory, and acoustic radiation. Spherical harmonics are fundamental to quantum mechanics, gravitational field modeling (GPS/geodesy), and climate modeling.

## Implementation approach
- spherical_jn/yn: use recurrence relation from ordinary Bessel. j_0(z) = sin(z)/z, j_1(z) = sin(z)/z² - cos(z)/z
- Forward recurrence for j_n, backward recurrence for y_n (stability considerations)
- lpmv: use recurrence in m then v (careful about normalization)
- sph_harm_y: Y_l^m = normalization * P_l^|m|(cos θ) * {cos(mφ), sin(mφ)}

## Dependencies
- Uses existing Bessel functions jv, yv from fsci-special
- Uses eval_legendre from orthogonal polynomial bead

## Testing requirements
- j_0(x) = sin(x)/x identity
- j_n(0) = δ_{n,0}
- Spherical harmonics: orthonormality ∫Y_l^m Y_l'^{m'} dΩ = δ_{ll'} δ_{mm'}
- Recurrence: (2n+1) j_n(z) = z(j_{n-1}(z) + j_{n+1}(z))
- At least 8 tests
close_reason: Implemented spherical Bessel functions (spherical_jn, spherical_yn, spherical_in, spherical_kn), associated Legendre function (lpmv), complex spherical harmonics (sph_harm), and real spherical harmonics (sph_harm_y). 23 new tests, all passing.

title: [HIGH] conformance: THREE parallel conformance entry-point hierarchies (run_<family>_packet x10, run_linalg_packet_with_oracle_capture x1, run_differential_<family> x12) — only 2 of ~23 entries actually invoke a scipy oracle
description:
Review of crates/fsci-conformance/src/lib.rs on 2026-04-22:

Three parallel conformance entry-point hierarchies exist:

Hierarchy A -- Self-check packet runners (pub fn run_<family>_packet):
  1553 run_validate_tol_packet    (self-check vs fixture expected)
  1631 run_linalg_packet           (self-check)
  1721 run_optimize_packet         (self-check)
  1755 run_special_packet          (self-check)
  1788 run_array_api_packet        (self-check)
  1821 run_sparse_packet           (self-check)
  2090 run_fft_packet              (self-check)
  2660 run_casp_packet             (self-check)
  3244 run_cluster_packet          (self-check)
  3945 run_spatial_packet          (self-check)

  MISSING: run_stats_packet, run_signal_packet, run_integrate_packet,
  run_ndimage_packet, run_interpolate_packet, run_io_packet (crates exist
  but no packet runner).

Hierarchy B -- Oracle-backed capture (pub fn run_<family>_packet_with_oracle_capture):
  5973 run_linalg_packet_with_oracle_capture   (ONLY oracle-invoking entry)

Hierarchy C -- 'Differential' runners invoked by run_differential_test:
  Lines 7719-8000 -- 12 functions, one per family (ivg5)
  Per ivg5: only run_differential_stats attempts the oracle; others are
  self-check despite the 'differential' name.

Total: ~23 conformance entry points across 3 hierarchies. Scipy oracle
is invoked by exactly TWO of them:
  - run_linalg_packet_with_oracle_capture (from tests only; not from
    run_differential_test dispatch)
  - run_differential_stats (from run_differential_test, only since today's
    scipy_stats_oracle.py landed per cpgl)

The architecture conveys intent to support oracle backing at multiple
layers but the implementation is extremely sparse. Users / reviewers
have no way to tell which entry point does what without reading source.

Impact:
1. Users calling run_linalg_packet get self-check results; users calling
   run_linalg_packet_with_oracle_capture get oracle-backed. Same
   function-looking names, different semantics. Easy to use the wrong one.
2. The differential_<family>_quota_and_structured_logs tests all call
   run_differential_test, which means they all self-check (ivg5). The
   oracle-backed run_linalg_packet_with_oracle_capture is tested only
   from run_linalg_packet_with_oracle_capture test cases.
3. Conformance dashboard (7h9o) surfaces parity_report.json from whichever
   entry wrote it last -- could be self-check or oracle, no way to tell.

Fix:
1. Deprecate run_<family>_packet (self-check only); require all public
   entries to either invoke oracle (required: true default) or be named
   run_<family>_self_check.
2. Consolidate Hierarchy B and Hierarchy C: run_differential_test should
   dispatch to the with-oracle-capture variant when oracle is available.
3. Add missing runners for stats/signal/integrate/ndimage/interpolate/io.
4. Publish a single public entry point with explicit oracle-or-self-check
   flag:
     pub fn run_conformance(fixture, ConformanceConfig{oracle_policy: Required/Optional/Self}).
5. Maintain per-family typed dispatch via ubkg-enum (not substring match).

Related: ivg5 (run_differential_test isn't differential), cpgl (stats
oracle landed today), di9p (10 missing oracles), 7h9o (dashboard
displays uniformly), ubkg (substring family dispatch), 95ld (String mode
field dead). The architecture is consistently ambitious and consistently
self-referential.

Current failing sites include:

- `log_cosh` at [4121](/data/projects/frankenscipy/crates/fsci-special/src/convenience.rs:4121)

title: [HIGH] conformance: 5 families (spatial/integrate/stats/cluster/signal) have 100% contract_ref fixture coverage but NO contract_table loader in the runner — 116 contract_ref values are dead metadata
description:
Review of crates/fsci-conformance/src/lib.rs + fixtures/ on 2026-04-22:

Contract-table loader functions (lib.rs:7297-7332):
    load_optimize_contract_table  -> loads P2C-003/contracts/contract_table.json  (file EXISTS)
    load_special_contract_table   -> loads P2C-006/contracts/contract_table.json  (file MISSING per r575)
    load_array_api_contract_table -> loads P2C-007/contracts/contract_table.json  (file EXISTS)

Fixture contract_ref coverage (grep of expected.contract_ref presence):
    P2C-003 optimize  31/39 cases have contract_ref; loader exists; file exists -> works
    P2C-006 special   232/257 cases; loader exists; file MISSING -> silent default (r575)
    P2C-007 arrayapi  13/29 cases; loader exists; file exists -> works
    P2C-010 spatial    26/26 cases have contract_ref; NO LOADER exists
    P2C-013 integrate  28/28 cases have contract_ref; NO LOADER exists
    P2C-012 stats      30/30 cases have contract_ref; NO LOADER exists
    P2C-009 cluster    20/20 cases have contract_ref; NO LOADER exists
    P2C-011 signal     12/12 cases have contract_ref; NO LOADER exists

For the 5 families (spatial/integrate/stats/cluster/signal), the contract_ref field in every fixture case is DEAD METADATA. The runner's comparator pulls atol/rtol from the fixture's explicit atol/rtol fields (which default to 1e-10 or 1e-9 per family) -- the contract_ref string is deserialized into the expected_outcome struct and then silently ignored.

116 cases (26+28+30+20+12) carry a contract_ref value that has NO consuming code path. This creates:
1. A maintenance tax: developers updating a fixture case will spend time choosing a meaningful contract_ref value, not realizing it has no effect.
2. An illusion of sophisticated tolerance handling when reviewers see the field.
3. A silent-failure vector: a fixture case labeled 'contract_ref: ill_conditioned_solve' will use the default tight atol=1e-10, flagging correct-but-imprecise outputs as conformance failures.

Ask:
1. Add missing loaders for spatial, integrate, stats, cluster, signal. Each follows the load_<family>_contract_table pattern (static OnceLock + read_to_string + serde_json::from_str).
2. Populate the corresponding fixtures/artifacts/P2C-XXX/contracts/contract_table.json files with per-function tolerance policies. Seed from scipy's own rtol conventions where available.
3. OR if contract tables aren't planned for these 5 families, STRIP the contract_ref fields from their fixtures to prevent confusion. Silent-metadata-with-no-effect is worse than no-metadata.
4. Add a CI validator: for every fixture case with contract_ref set, assert a loader exists for that family.

Related: r575 (special contract table missing even though loader exists -- the other failure mode), 37ip (tolerance contract gap), ivg5 (comparator uses fixture atol/rtol directly so when contract_ref is ignored, tolerance defaults silently).

title: EPIC: Stats Scope-Up (describe, tests, distributions)
description:
## Background

fsci-stats at 19% coverage has 14 distributions + basic hypothesis tests. FEATURE_PARITY.md #1 highest-impact missing: describe() + skew + kurtosis + mode. Also missing: ~100+ distributions, KS tests, Shapiro, Mann-Whitney, KDE, boxcox, zscore, kendalltau, entropy, gaussian_kde. Memory claimed "24+ distributions" but actual count is 14 — memory was wrong.

## Goal

Implement the highest-impact stats functions: describe(), skew, kurtosis, mode, sem, iqr, variation; add KS tests (ks_1samp, ks_2samp); add 10-15 more commonly used distributions (Binomial, NegBinomial, Geometric, Pareto, Rayleigh, etc.).

## Success Criteria

1. describe() returning nobs, mean, variance, skewness, kurtosis, min, max
2. skew(), kurtosis(), mode(), sem(), iqr(), variation() standalone functions
3. ks_1samp, ks_2samp Kolmogorov-Smirnov tests
4. 10+ additional distributions with pdf/cdf/ppf/rvs
5. Unit tests + conformance fixtures
6. FEATURE_PARITY.md updated with actual distribution count

## Serves Project Goal

stats.describe is the #1 highest-impact missing function across ALL of FrankenSciPy per FEATURE_PARITY.md.

title: Implement describe(), skew, kurtosis, mode, sem, iqr, variation
description:
## What

Implement the core descriptive statistics functions:
- describe(a) → DescribeResult { nobs, minmax, mean, variance, skewness, kurtosis }
- skew(a, bias) → sample skewness (Fisher's definition)
- kurtosis(a, fisher, bias) → excess kurtosis (Fisher) or regular (Pearson)
- mode(a) → most frequent value(s)
- sem(a) → standard error of mean
- iqr(a) → interquartile range (Q3 - Q1)
- variation(a) → coefficient of variation (std/mean)

## Why

describe() is the #1 highest-impact missing function across ALL of FrankenSciPy per FEATURE_PARITY.md. These are the bread-and-butter stats functions that every data analysis workflow uses first.

## Implementation Notes

- skew: m3 / m2^(3/2), with bias correction option
- kurtosis: m4 / m2² - 3 (Fisher), or m4 / m2² (Pearson)
- mode: sort and count runs, or HashMap-based
- iqr: percentile(75) - percentile(25), need quantile computation
- NaN handling: nan_policy parameter (propagate, raise, omit)

## Tests

- describe([1,2,3,4,5]) → known values
- skew of symmetric distribution → ~0
- kurtosis of normal → ~0 (excess) or ~3 (Pearson)
- mode([1,1,2,2,3]) → [1,2] (multi-modal)
- sem of constant array → 0
- NaN handling for each function
notes: Functions already implemented: describe, skew, kurtosis, mode, sem, iqr, variation all exist in lib.rs with tests
close_reason: already implemented: describe, skew, kurtosis, mode, sem, iqr, variation all in fsci-stats (verified in reality-check)

title: Implement ks_1samp, ks_2samp Kolmogorov-Smirnov tests
description:
## What

Implement ks_1samp(x, cdf) — one-sample KS test against a reference CDF, and ks_2samp(data1, data2) — two-sample KS test. Return: KS statistic D, p-value.

## Why

#10 highest-impact missing function. KS tests are the standard nonparametric goodness-of-fit test. Essential for distribution fitting validation.

## Implementation Notes

- ks_1samp: compute empirical CDF of x, find max absolute deviation from reference CDF, compute p-value via Kolmogorov distribution
- ks_2samp: merge sorted samples, compute max deviation between two empirical CDFs
- P-value: Kolmogorov-Smirnov distribution using asymptotic formula (Kolmogorov 1933) or exact for small n
- Alternative parameter: two-sided, less, greater

## Tests

- Normal sample vs Normal CDF → high p-value (fail to reject)
- Normal sample vs Uniform CDF → low p-value (reject)
- Two identical samples → D≈0, high p-value
- Known-answer cases from statistics textbooks
close_reason: already implemented: ks_1samp, ks_2samp in fsci-stats (verified in reality-check)

title: Add 10+ distributions (Binomial, NegBinomial, Geometric, Pareto, Rayleigh, etc.)
description:
## What

Implement the next tier of most-used distributions. Each needs: pdf/pmf, cdf, ppf/isf, rvs (random sampling), mean/var/std, entropy. Priority order:

Continuous: Pareto, Rayleigh, Gumbel, Logistic, InverseGaussian, Chi
Discrete: Binomial, NegativeBinomial, Geometric, Hypergeometric

## Why

fsci-stats claims 14 distributions but SciPy has 100+. These 10 are among the most commonly used and would bring us from 14 → 24+ actual distributions (correcting the memory's overcount).

## Implementation Notes

- Use the existing Distribution trait pattern established by the 14 current implementations
- Each distribution: validate parameters, implement pdf/cdf/ppf analytically where possible
- ppf: use the analytical PPF pattern established in recent commits (not bisection-based)
- rvs: inverse transform sampling via ppf(uniform(0,1)) or specialized samplers
- Discrete distributions: use pmf not pdf, cdf as sum, ppf via search

## Tests

- Each distribution: pdf/cdf at known points, ppf(cdf(x)) ≈ x round-trip
- Mean and variance match theoretical values
- rvs sample mean/var converge to theoretical (statistical test)
- Edge cases: extreme parameters, boundary values

title: EPIC: Real Differential Oracle Conformance
description:
## Background

SPEC §8 mandates differential conformance: generate canonical fixture corpus, run LEGACY ORACLE (SciPy) and capture normalized outputs, run FrankenSciPy and compare under explicit equality/tolerance policy, produce machine-readable parity report. README §"Conformance Workflow" and "Next Steps #2" call for a "SciPy-present CI lane for real oracle capture."

## Current State (2026-04-09 Reality Check)

The conformance infrastructure is a SELF-CHECKING unit test layer dressed up as differential conformance:
- P2C-001..013 fixture files contain HAND-TYPED expected values in JSON
- Zero oracle_capture.json files exist anywhere in the repo
- PythonOracleConfig, OracleCapture, capture_linalg_oracle() exist as DEAD CODE — never called at test time
- "53/53 passing" on P2C-002 means "Rust matches the hand-coded JSON we created months ago"
- Drift detection (SciPy bumps tolerance, fixes bug, changes warning class) CANNOT be detected by current harness

## Goal

Stand up REAL differential conformance: a CI lane that pip-installs SciPy, runs the oracle capture script, generates oracle_capture.json with provenance hashes, and diffs FrankenSciPy's output against it. Replace hand-typed expected values with oracle-captured values for all P2C packets.

## Success Criteria

1. oracle_capture.json generated by actual SciPy subprocess for P2C-002 (linalg)
2. Conformance harness loads oracle_capture.json and compares Rust output against it
3. Process works for P2C-001 (integrate), P2C-003 (opt), P2C-005 (FFT), P2C-006 (special)
4. CI pipeline (or documented manual workflow) regenerates oracle captures on demand
5. Provenance hash (blake3 of SciPy version + input + output) stored per capture

## Serves Project Goal

Without this, "differential conformance against SciPy legacy oracle" is fiction. Every "parity_green" status in FEATURE_PARITY.md is unproven. This epic makes conformance real.

title: Activate Python oracle capture for P2C-002 (linalg)
description:
## What

Make the existing (but dead) PythonOracleConfig / capture_linalg_oracle() machinery ACTUALLY RUN during conformance tests. Generate a real oracle_capture.json for P2C-002 by invoking the Python SciPy linalg oracle script (crates/fsci-conformance/python_oracle/scipy_linalg_oracle.py).

## Why

The oracle capture code exists but is never called. It was built during the MASTER_EXECUTION_TODO work but the test runner still compares against hand-typed expected values in fixture JSON. We need to close the loop: run SciPy, capture output, compare Rust against it.

## Implementation Notes

- The oracle script at python_oracle/scipy_linalg_oracle.py already exists and is implemented
- PythonOracleConfig, OracleCapture, run_linalg_packet_with_oracle_capture() exist in fsci-conformance
- Need to: (1) add a test target that invokes capture, (2) skip gracefully when SciPy is not installed (#[ignore] or runtime skip), (3) write oracle_capture.json to FSCI-P2C-002/ artifact dir
- Record provenance: blake3 hash of SciPy version + numpy version + fixture input + oracle output

## Tests

- Test that oracle_capture.json is generated (when SciPy available)
- Test that provenance hash is recorded
- Test that Rust results match oracle_capture values within tolerance policy

## Files to modify

- crates/fsci-conformance/src/lib.rs (activate capture path)
- crates/fsci-conformance/tests/ (new oracle capture test)
close_reason: Implemented oracle capture test for P2C-002. Test invokes scipy_linalg_oracle.py, generates oracle_capture.json with provenance hashes, skips gracefully when SciPy unavailable.

title: Replace hand-typed P2C-002 expected values with oracle-captured values

## What

Replace the hard-coded "expected.values" in FSCI-P2C-002_linalg_core.json fixture file with values from oracle_capture.json. The fixture file should reference the oracle capture rather than containing hand-typed numbers.

## Why

The current 53 expected values were hand-typed or computed once. They have no provenance and cannot be regenerated. After this change, expected values come from SciPy with a provenance hash, and can be refreshed when SciPy updates.

## Implementation Notes

- Option A: Fixture JSON references oracle_capture.json, harness loads both
- Option B: A regeneration script reads oracle_capture.json and updates fixture expected values, recording provenance
- Option B preferred: keeps the fixture file self-contained while adding a regen workflow
- Provenance field added to each case: oracle_hash, scipy_version, capture_timestamp

## Dependencies

- Requires: oracle capture activated for P2C-002

## Files to modify

## Why

title: [linalg] Special matrix constructors: toeplitz, circulant, hilbert, hadamard, companion, block_diag
description:
## Goal
Implement special matrix construction functions. These structured matrices appear throughout numerical linear algebra, signal processing, and combinatorics.

## What to implement
- `toeplitz(c, r)`: Toeplitz matrix (constant along diagonals). Fundamental in signal processing (convolution ↔ Toeplitz matrix-vector product).
- `circulant(c)`: circulant matrix (Toeplitz with periodic wrap). Diagonalized by FFT.
- `hilbert(n)`: Hilbert matrix H_{ij} = 1/(i+j+1). Classic ill-conditioned test matrix.
- `invhilbert(n)`: exact inverse of Hilbert matrix (integer entries).
- `hadamard(n)`: Hadamard matrix (±1 entries, H^T H = nI). Used in error-correcting codes and compressed sensing. n must be power of 2.
- `companion(a)`: companion matrix of polynomial with coefficients a. Eigenvalues = roots of the polynomial.
- `block_diag(*arrs)`: block diagonal matrix from input matrices.

## Testing requirements
- toeplitz: verify constant diagonals, toeplitz(c) is symmetric when r is None
- circulant: eigenvalues are FFT of first column
- hilbert: known condition number grows exponentially with n
- hadamard: H^T @ H = n * I
- companion: eigenvalues match numpy.roots of the polynomial
- block_diag: correct size, blocks on diagonal
- At least 10 tests
close_reason: Implemented toeplitz, circulant, hilbert, invhilbert, hadamard, companion, block_diag with 11 tests

title: [HIGH] conformance: fsci-stats has no scipy_stats_oracle.py — e2e_stats.rs is self-checking not oracle-backed
description:
Review-mode audit on 2026-04-22 found crates/fsci-conformance/python_oracle/ has oracles for fft, linalg, optimize, sparse, special — but NO scipy_stats_oracle.py. Meanwhile crates/fsci-conformance/tests/e2e_stats.rs is 134 KB / 95 test functions but all self-checking: expected values are hand-typed floats inside the .rs file, not pulled from SciPy.

This violates the /testing-conformance-harnesses differential pattern: without a reference oracle, 'conformant' means 'agrees with whatever we hand-wrote'. Bug classes like t2wx/s6kn/gwfm slip past because the test matches the implementation, not SciPy.

Ask:
1. Add crates/fsci-conformance/python_oracle/scipy_stats_oracle.py mirroring the structure of scipy_special_oracle.py — reads a fixture JSON (case_id, distribution, params, method, args), invokes scipy.stats.<dist>.<method>(*args), emits normalized oracle capture JSON with relative-error context.
2. Seed fixture coverage: every dist × {pdf, cdf, ppf, sf, logpdf, logcdf, mean, var, entropy} × a handful of (loc, scale) values + edge inputs (NaN/inf/domain-boundary).
3. Add an e2e_stats oracle-backed test alongside the existing self-checking one; XFAIL any known divergences in a new DISCREPANCIES.md.
4. Wire into fixture provenance: record scipy version + numpy version when regenerating.

Related beads 3dg8 and j3ln already track mstats-helper-specific coverage; this covers the distribution surface more broadly.
close_reason: Added scipy_stats_oracle.py plus oracle-backed stats differential comparison and mock-backed regression coverage in fsci-conformance.

title: [HIGH] conformance: blake3 provenance hashes are captured but NEVER CHECKED — runner cannot detect stale oracle_capture.json when fixture or scipy version drifts
description:
Review of crates/fsci-conformance/src/lib.rs on 2026-04-22:

The runner has a fully-implemented provenance mechanism:
- OracleCaptureProvenance struct at line 1466 with fixture_input_blake3, oracle_output_blake3, capture_blake3.
- attach_oracle_capture_provenance at line 6085 computes all three hashes when capture_oracle runs (line 6063).
- Hashes cover: fixture bytes + oracle output bytes + scipy/numpy/python versions (line 6094-6100).

But the runner NEVER VERIFIES the hashes on load.
Verification path: grep -nE 'provenance\.fixture_input_blake3|provenance\.capture_blake3' crates/fsci-conformance/src/lib.rs
Only matches: lines 11409/11411 inside a *test* that asserts non-empty. Zero matches in run_differential_test or load_oracle_capture (line 6074 which just parses JSON).

Consequence (same class as m76c but now at PROTOCOL level):
- Edit the fixture JSON (add a new case, change an arg, fix a typo) WITHOUT re-running the oracle: oracle_capture.json still has the OLD fixture_input_blake3. The runner loads the old oracle values and compares them against Rust outputs for cases that may not exist in the old capture. Those cases silently skip OR get compared against wrong (older) oracle values.
- Upgrade scipy 1.17 -> 1.18 with e.g. a bug fix that changes scipy.special.gammainc for some edge case: oracle_capture.json records scipy_version='1.17.1' in runtime but the runner doesn't know or care; Rust conformance 'passes' against stale reference values.
- Modify scipy_special_oracle.py (e.g., to fix stirling2 per m9jp): oracle_capture.json wasn't regenerated, but there's no way for the runner to know the script changed.

Fix:
1. In run_differential_test / run_differential_<family>, after loading oracle_capture.json:
   a. Recompute blake3 of the CURRENT fixture input bytes.
   b. Compare against capture.provenance.fixture_input_blake3.
   c. If mismatch -> HarnessError::StaleCapture with instruction to regenerate.
2. Similarly compare current scipy/python/numpy versions (from a new probe_current_versions()) to capture.runtime -- if MAJOR version drifts, require --force-stale flag to override.
3. Extend capture_blake3 to include a hash of the oracle SCRIPT (python_oracle/scipy_<family>_oracle.py) -- so script edits also invalidate captures.
4. Add DISCREPANCIES.md (per ma1a) entries for any intentionally-tolerated version drift.

This is the infrastructure change that unlocks fail-closed fixture staleness. Right now the mechanism is present but inert.

Related: m76c (specific 9-day-stale FSCI-P2C-006), ma1a (no DISCREPANCIES.md), 37ip (tolerance contract). The existing probe_oracle_availability (line 10947) only checks scipy imports -- it doesn't check version, and that's a separate deficiency.

title: [HIGH] conformance: FSCI-P2C-006 fixture has only 8/257 cases (3.1%) with complex-structured args — complex-domain differential coverage is shallow
description:
Review-mode audit of crates/fsci-conformance/fixtures/FSCI-P2C-006_special_core.json:

- 257 total cases
- 8 cases (3.1%) pass complex-structured args (dict with 're'/'im' keys or lists thereof)

The ratio of fixture coverage (3.1%) to source complex-handling (90% of modules) is profoundly imbalanced.

Impact:
- Branch-cut bugs (like gamma at nonpositive integers, lambertw at -1/e, log/sqrt on negative real axis) are the #1 correctness risk for a complex-domain special-fn library. 3.1% fixture coverage cannot possibly exercise these.
- The recent 'fuzz target for elliptic_complex' (p2c006_special_elliptic_complex.rs) is the RIGHT response for elliptic but not scaled across all 10 modules.
- Combined with frankenscipy-v2tz (64.6% of cases reference unsupported oracle functions), the effective complex-domain differential coverage is ≤ 3% × 36% ≈ 1.1%.

Ask:
1. Add 30-50 new complex-args cases to FSCI-P2C-006_special_core.json distributed over gamma, bessel (jv/yv/iv/kv/hankel1/hankel2), airy, erf/erfc/wrightomega, hyp2f1, lambertw — each with at least 3 sample z values on/near the canonical branch cut. Use the {re, im} dict encoding already supported by _decode_complex_pair.
2. For functions where scipy returns complex results (e.g. hankel1, hankel2, sqrt-of-negative), ensure result_kind='complex_scalar' roundtrip is exercised.
3. Tie into frankenscipy-tmti: every new Rust complex fuzz target should have matching oracle-backed conformance cases for regression capture.

title: [CRITICAL] conformance: 25 inline 'atol + rtol * x.abs()' tolerance sites replicate allclose_scalar i0hm inf bug AND diverge from allclose_scalar NaN convention
description:
Review of crates/fsci-conformance/src/lib.rs on 2026-04-22:

grep 'atol \+ rtol \* ' finds 25 inline-copied tolerance-math sites across the comparator:
  cluster outcome     3084, 3109, 3138, 3181, 3209
  signal outcome      3570, 3599, 3629
  spatial outcome     3657, 3679, 3697
  (spatial variant)   3775, 3893, 3937
  stats outcome       4274, ...
  (more below 4274)

Each site implements:
    let tol = atol + rtol * expected.abs();
    let pass = (actual - expected).abs() <= tol;

Two bugs replicated from i0hm at every site:

Bug 1 -- Infinity equality inverted (same as i0hm):
    (+inf, +inf): (inf-inf).abs() = NaN; NaN <= tol is FALSE.  Genuine match rejected.
    (+inf, -inf): (inf-(-inf)).abs() = inf; inf <= atol + rtol*inf = inf is TRUE.  Sign flip accepted.

Bug 2 -- NaN convention diverges from allclose_scalar:
    allclose_scalar at 6950 treats NaN == NaN -> match (returns true).
    Inline math: (NaN - NaN).abs() = NaN; NaN <= tol is FALSE -> reject.

So the SAME pair (NaN, NaN) returns PASS through allclose_scalar but FAIL through 25 inline comparators. Divergent semantics within one comparator suite.

Impact:
- Every time a special/stats function legitimately returns +inf (gamma pole, 1/0+), cluster/signal/spatial/etc. comparator REJECTS the match.
- Every sign-flip bug (Rust returns +inf, scipy returns -inf) across cluster/signal/spatial is AUTO-ACCEPTED.
- NaN propagation tests behave one way in allclose_scalar lanes and another way in inline lanes. Fixture cases move between consistent states by accident.

Fix:
1. Centralize: replace every inline occurrence with . Fix the inf semantics in allclose_scalar per i0hm.
2. Same-hash grep: ensure no new copies introduced (44 must equal 0 post-fix, all via allclose_scalar).
3. Add a Rust unit test to the runner:
      assert!(allclose_scalar(f64::INFINITY, f64::INFINITY, 1e-8, 1e-5));
      assert!(!allclose_scalar(f64::INFINITY, f64::NEG_INFINITY, 1e-8, 1e-5));
      assert!(allclose_scalar(f64::NAN, f64::NAN, 1e-8, 1e-5) == <documented convention>);
4. Declare NaN convention in DISCREPANCIES.md (per ma1a).

Related: i0hm (primary bug in allclose_scalar), ihex (both-errored auto-pass), 37ip (tolerance contract gap), r575 (special contract table missing).

title: [HIGH] correctness: complex_gammaln/complex_digamma_scalar silently return Complex(-inf, ...) or NaN at integer poles instead of failing closed
description:
Review of crates/fsci-special/src/gamma.rs:1893-1965:

1. complex_gammaln at z = (-n, 0) for n = 0, 1, 2, ... (non-positive integers, which are poles of Γ):
   - Line 1894 takes reflection branch (z.re < 0.5).
   - Line 1898 computes sin(π*z). For z = (0, 0): sin(0) = 0. For z = (-1, 0): sin(-π) = 0.
   - Line 1899 computes sin_pi_z.ln() — ln(0 + 0i) in num-complex gives Complex(-inf, NaN or 0).
   - The final result is Complex(∞, something or NaN) silently.

2. complex_digamma_scalar at z = (-n, 0) for n = 0, 1, 2, ...:
   - Line 1948 takes reflection branch.
   - Line 1951 computes π * cot(π*z). cot(π*(-1)) = cos(-π)/sin(-π) = -1/0 → divergent.
   - Returns Complex(inf, NaN) or similar silently.

Both functions DO NOT honor the fail-closed doctrine at their canonical singularity points. The real-axis analogues (gamma_scalar via rgamma / gammaln_scalar) have explicit pole checks:

    if x <= 0.0 && x == x.floor() { return Ok(f64::INFINITY) or domain_error() }

but the complex branches skip this.

Impact:
- Fuzz targets using z with z.im = 0 and z.re a negative integer will silently accept garbage outputs.
- Downstream callers (complex rgamma = 1/gamma) depend on complex_gammaln; rgamma should return 0 at negative integers (rgamma(poles) = 0 is the defining identity). Our impl likely returns exp(-inf) = 0 by luck, or NaN.
- The documentation says these functions handle all of C, but they silently violate contract at countably many points.

Fix:
1. In complex_gammaln: before reflection, check if z.im == 0 AND z.re <= 0 AND z.re.fract() == 0.0 → return Complex(-inf + iπ*n, 0) for proper branch tracking, OR domain_error per mode.
2. In complex_digamma_scalar: same check — at non-positive integers, ψ has simple poles. Return Complex(inf, 0) or domain_error.
3. For nearby-but-not-exactly integer z (e.g., z = (-1.0, 1e-30)), ensure the reflection formula doesn't produce pathological values — may need a different formula near poles.
4. Add regression tests:
       for n in [0, -1, -2, -5, -10]:
           assert!(complex_gammaln((n, 0)).re.is_infinite() || is_domain_error(n, mode=Hardened));
           assert!(rgamma_scalar_complex((n, 0)) == 0.0);  # rgamma = 0 at gamma poles

Related: 276r (complex_sqrt -0 bug), tmti (new complex fuzz harness), 0acr (fuzz EdgeF64 gamma poles).
close_reason: Returned NaN at exact complex gamma/digamma poles and preserved rgamma zero-at-poles

title: EPIC: Signal Scope-Up (9% → 30%+)
description:
## Background

fsci-signal is at 9% SciPy coverage — the LOWEST of any non-trivial module. FEATURE_PARITY.md lists it as in_progress. Implemented: savgol, hann/hamming/blackman/kaiser windows, convolve, fftconvolve, find_peaks, butter, lfilter, filtfilt, periodogram, welch. Missing (HIGH priority): cheby1/cheby2/ellip/bessel (IIR design), firwin/remez/kaiserord (FIR design), sosfilt/sosfiltfilt/tf2sos/sos2tf (SOS filtering), freqz/freqs/group_delay (frequency response), spectrogram/stft/istft/csd/coherence (spectral), resample/decimate (resampling), chirp/sawtooth/square (waveforms).

## Goal

Bring signal to ≥30% coverage by implementing the highest-impact missing families: IIR filter design (cheby1/cheby2/ellip/bessel), FIR design (firwin), frequency response (freqz), SOS filtering (sosfilt/sosfiltfilt/tf2sos), and time-frequency analysis (spectrogram/stft).

## Success Criteria

1. cheby1, cheby2, ellip, bessel filter design functions
2. firwin FIR filter design
3. freqz frequency response computation
4. sosfilt, sosfiltfilt, tf2sos, sos2tf conversions
5. spectrogram, stft (forward only for V1)
6. Unit tests + conformance fixtures for each
7. FEATURE_PARITY.md updated

## Serves Project Goal

Signal at 9% is embarrassing for a project claiming V1 scope includes "signal families." These are the 10 most commonly used scipy.signal functions we lack.

title: Implement IIR filter design: cheby1, cheby2, ellip, bessel
description:
## What

Implement the four missing IIR filter design functions: cheby1 (Chebyshev Type I), cheby2 (Chebyshev Type II), ellip (elliptic/Cauer), bessel (Bessel/Thomson). All should accept: order, critical frequencies, btype (lowpass/highpass/bandpass/bandstop), and return transfer function (b, a) or zpk form.

## Why

butter() is implemented but real-world DSP requires sharper rolloff (cheby1/2, ellip) or maximally flat group delay (bessel). These are HIGH priority in FEATURE_PARITY.md.

## Implementation Notes

- Prototype-to-filter workflow: compute analog prototype poles/zeros → frequency transform → bilinear transform to digital
- butter() already implements this pipeline — cheby1/2/ellip/bessel need different prototype generation
- Cheby1: poles on ellipse in s-plane, ripple parameter
- Cheby2: zeros on jω axis, attenuation parameter
- Ellip: both poles and zeros, ripple + attenuation parameters
- Bessel: maximally flat group delay polynomial roots

## Tests

- Each filter type at order 4, lowpass, known cutoff → compare transfer function coefficients against SciPy
- Bandpass and bandstop variants
- Order estimation functions (optional, lower priority)
notes: Already implemented in fsci-signal

title: Implement freqz, sosfilt, tf2sos, firwin
description:
## What

Implement four critical signal processing utilities:
1. freqz(b, a, worN) — frequency response of digital filter. Returns (w, h) complex frequency response.
2. sosfilt(sos, x) — filter data with cascaded second-order sections. More numerically stable than lfilter for high-order filters.
3. tf2sos(b, a) — convert transfer function to second-order sections.
4. firwin(numtaps, cutoff, window, pass_zero) — FIR filter design using windowed sinc method.

## Why

freqz is #6 on FEATURE_PARITY highest-impact list. sosfilt/tf2sos are the numerically stable filtering pathway. firwin is #8 highest-impact. These four functions are needed by almost every DSP workflow.

## Implementation Notes

- freqz: evaluate H(z) = B(z)/A(z) at points on unit circle. Use FFT for efficiency when worN is integer.
- sosfilt: direct-form II transposed, cascaded 2nd-order sections
- tf2sos: root-finding on b/a → pair conjugate poles/zeros → form sections
- firwin: windowed ideal lowpass, use existing window functions (hann, hamming, etc.)

## Tests

- freqz of butter lowpass → known magnitude response shape
- sosfilt matches lfilter output for well-conditioned filters
- tf2sos round-trip: sos2tf(tf2sos(b,a)) ≈ (b,a)
- firwin at various orders → verify passband/stopband via freqz
notes: Already implemented in fsci-signal

title: Implement spectrogram and stft
description:
## What

Implement spectrogram(x, fs, window, nperseg, noverlap) and stft(x, fs, window, nperseg, noverlap). spectrogram returns (f, t, Sxx) power spectral density matrix. stft returns (f, t, Zxx) complex STFT matrix.

## Why

spectrogram and stft are #7 on FEATURE_PARITY highest-impact list. Time-frequency analysis is fundamental for audio, vibration, and biomedical signal processing.

## Implementation Notes

- Both are built on segmented FFT: divide signal into overlapping windows, FFT each, collect columns
- spectrogram = |stft|² (power spectral density or power spectrum depending on mode)
- Use existing fft() and window functions from fsci-fft/fsci-signal
- Segment parameters: nperseg, noverlap, nfft, detrend option

## Tests

- Known sinusoid → peak at correct frequency bin in spectrogram
- stft with rectangular window, no overlap → matches plain FFT of each segment
- Parseval's theorem: sum of spectrogram ≈ signal energy
notes: Already implemented in fsci-signal

title: [CRITICAL] conformance: 10 of 15 scientific families have NO scipy oracle at all — signal/stats/spatial/cluster/interpolate/integrate/ndimage/io/arrayapi/constants are entirely self-checking
description:
Review-mode audit on 2026-04-22 of crates/fsci-conformance/python_oracle/:

Existing oracles (5):     scipy_fft_oracle.py, scipy_linalg_oracle.py, scipy_optimize_oracle.py, scipy_sparse_oracle.py, scipy_special_oracle.py
MISSING oracles (10):     signal, stats, spatial, cluster, interpolate, integrate, ndimage, io, arrayapi, constants

But every one of these 10 families has:
- A Cargo crate under crates/fsci-<family>/ with real numerical code
- A conformance packet fixture under crates/fsci-conformance/fixtures/FSCI-P2C-XXX_<family>_core.json
- An e2e test file under crates/fsci-conformance/tests/e2e_<family>.rs (typically 50KB-134KB of scenarios)
- A packet_runner in crates/fsci-conformance/src/lib.rs that runs those fixtures and reports 'passes'

With no scipy oracle wired, the packet runners can only:
(a) Compare against values hard-coded in the Rust fixture file (e.g. 'expected': 1.23 hand-typed) -- not scipy parity, just internal consistency.
(b) Call 'differential_<family>_quota_and_structured_logs' tests (observed in src/lib.rs) which check that quota gates are met without touching scipy.

The conformance dashboard reports high parity numbers for these families, but the numbers are NOT actually differential vs scipy -- they're self-check scores. This is the same systemic illusion flagged in v2tz (35.4% effective oracle coverage for special) but 100% illusory for 10 whole families.

The already-filed cpgl (fsci-stats no scipy_stats_oracle.py) covers the stats slice specifically. This bead covers the SYSTEMIC gap.

Ask (prioritized by user impact):
1. signal: port the hilbert dispatcher out of scipy_fft_oracle.py (where it's misplaced per spm3), plus create full scipy_signal_oracle.py covering the 32 scenarios in e2e_signal.rs -- use scipy.signal.* functions directly.
2. stats: scipy_stats_oracle.py per cpgl (already filed; marked [HIGH]).
3. integrate: scipy_integrate_oracle.py for IVP / quadrature / cubature -- leverage scipy.integrate.quad / solve_ivp as reference. The 11 scenarios in e2e_integrate.rs need oracle-backed regression.
4. spatial: distance functions (pdist, cdist, kdtree queries) -- scipy.spatial has all of these.
5. cluster: hierarchical linkage, fcluster, kmeans, dbscan -- scipy.cluster + sklearn (only for dbscan).
6. interpolate: interp1d, CubicSpline, PCHIP, RBF -- scipy.interpolate.
7. ndimage: filters, geometric transforms, morphology, labeling -- scipy.ndimage.
8. io: read_matrix_market / write_wav / read_csv -- scipy.io + pandas.
9. arrayapi: numpy is the natural reference; most operations are direct numpy calls.
10. constants: scipy.constants is ~100 named constants; oracle can be auto-generated from the module's __all__.

Also: update DISCREPANCIES.md (per ma1a) to record WHICH families currently lack oracle-backed conformance so users of the parity number aren't misled.

Related: cpgl (stats-specific slice), v2tz (special has oracle but 35% coverage), ma1a (no DISCREPANCIES.md), r9st (oracle gaps in special).

title: [HIGH] conformance: scipy_sparse_oracle.py implements 7 operations (find/tril/triu/tocsc/vstack/hstack/csr_matmul) but FSCI-P2C-004 fixture uses DIFFERENT 5 operations (spmv/spsolve/add/scale/format_roundtrip) — oracle never exercised
description:
Review-mode audit on 2026-04-22:

crates/fsci-conformance/python_oracle/scipy_sparse_oracle.py implements dispatchers for 7 operations:
    find, tril, triu, tocsc, vstack, hstack, csr_matmul

crates/fsci-conformance/fixtures/FSCI-P2C-004_sparse_ops.json (27 cases) uses:
    spsolve: 9, spmv: 6, format_roundtrip: 4, add: 4, scale: 4

Intersection: EMPTY. The sparse oracle and the sparse fixture have ZERO operations in common. Every case in FSCI-P2C-004_sparse_ops.json hits the fallback:
    return { 'status': 'error', 'result_kind': 'unsupported_operation', ... }

How does the conformance test still pass? Because the fixture has hand-typed expected.values per case:
    case0: operation='spmv', expected={'kind':'vector', 'values':[2.0, -1.0, 3.0], 'atol':1e-12, 'rtol':1e-10}

The Rust runner (crates/fsci-conformance/src/lib.rs:2007 compare_sparse_outcome) uses these hand-typed values directly. The scipy_sparse_oracle.py is NEVER consulted for this fixture. It's effectively dead code for the main sparse conformance lane.

Impact:
- Same illusion as di9p (10 missing oracles) but more insidious: here an oracle DOES exist, just for a different surface. Reviewers scanning the oracle directory see 'sparse_oracle.py' and assume parity is verified vs scipy. It is not.
- The hand-typed expected values are subject to the same class of error as scipy_special_oracle's stirling2 (m9jp) and scipy_optimize_oracle's mismatched gradients (pqka): one bad number in the fixture JSON silently becomes the 'correct' answer forever.
- No mechanism to cross-check the hand-typed values against scipy.

Two distinct problems to fix:
1. Extend scipy_sparse_oracle.py to cover spmv, spsolve, add, scale, format_roundtrip. All are one-line scipy.sparse calls. This closes the actual gap.
2. OR re-file the dead operations (find, tril, triu, etc.) as 'sparse_helpers' distinct from 'sparse_ops' and add a SECOND fixture FSCI-P2C-004b_sparse_helpers.json that actually uses them. This makes the existing oracle useful.
3. Replace hand-typed expected values in the primary fixture with oracle-generated ones via a 'just conformance-regen-sparse' target. This brings sparse in line with how special conformance was originally designed.

Also: audit fixture cases for silent operation-name typos (a fixture with operation='spmvv' would hit the same unsupported_operation path and conformance would still pass if hand-typed expected matched the Rust bug).

Related: di9p (10 missing oracles -- sparse is a 'stealth' eleventh), v2tz (special has 35% oracle coverage), m9jp/pqka (hand-typed math errors in other oracles), m76c (oracle staleness), 37ip (tolerance contract).

   infrastructure for future IIR filter work, evaluate whether to keep or remove.

## Pre-removal checklist

  without the __removed_ prefix
- Verify no code references the __removed_ functions

- Run full fsci-signal test suite before and after removal
- Verify no compilation errors or warnings
- Verify test count unchanged

## Validation
```
cargo test -p fsci-signal
cargo clippy -p fsci-signal --all-targets -- -D warnings
```

title: [HIGH] conformance: audit ledger emission is CRATE-WIDE gap — only fsci-linalg/fsci-fft/fsci-integrate emit AuditAction; fsci-special/fsci-stats/fsci-cluster/fsci-spatial/fsci-signal/fsci-sparse/fsci-opt/fsci-arrayapi have ZERO audit events, so even wiring ledger capture yields empty logs
description:
Review of crates/fsci-*/src/ AuditAction usage on 2026-04-22 (grep -rnE 'AuditAction::'):

Crates that EMIT audit events:
    fsci-runtime   (definitions + test sample emissions)
    fsci-linalg    (ModeDecision + BoundedRecovery + FailClosed)
    fsci-fft       (ModeDecision)
    fsci-integrate (FailClosed + BoundedRecovery)
    fsci-conformance (tests only)

Crates that DO NOT emit audit events:
    fsci-special       (340+ public fns, zero audit emissions)
    fsci-stats         (208 public fns)
    fsci-cluster
    fsci-spatial
    fsci-signal
    fsci-sparse        (despite having explicit Hardened validation)
    fsci-opt           (despite having validate_minimize_options)
    fsci-arrayapi
    fsci-interpolate
    fsci-ndimage
    fsci-io

This is DEEPER than mhuk (audit ledger emission wired for only 3 of 12
conformance runners). Even if mhuk is fixed and the conformance runner
instantiates a ledger for every family, the fsci-* crates WOULD WRITE
NOTHING because they don't emit AuditAction events.

Impact:
- 'Hardened conformance' claim (xo4o + 95ld) is hollow for 8 families
  beyond just the conformance-runner wiring gap.
- A Hardened-mode rejection in fsci-special (e.g., gamma at pole)
  SHOULD emit FailClosed. It doesn't. The conformance runner has nothing
  to assert about it.
- Failures that should be forensically analyzable (per forensics.rs
  classify_failure -- vp77) have no audit trail in the ledger even
  when ledgers ARE captured.

Separate smaller finding: AuditAction::PolicyOverride variant (defined
at fsci-runtime/evidence.rs:86) is NEVER emitted anywhere in crates/
(grep shows zero hits outside the enum definition). Dead variant.

Fix:
1. Audit each fsci-* crate's Hardened code paths and identify rejection
   / recovery events that should be recorded as AuditAction.
2. Add sync_audit_ledger() -> SharedAuditLedger analogs to fsci-special,
   fsci-stats, fsci-cluster, fsci-spatial, fsci-signal, fsci-sparse,
   fsci-opt, fsci-arrayapi matching the existing fsci-linalg / fsci-fft
   / fsci-integrate pattern.
3. Wire each rejection / recovery site in the 8 silent crates to
   ledger.record(AuditEvent::new(..., AuditAction::{FailClosed |
   BoundedRecovery | ModeDecision | PolicyOverride})).
4. Remove AuditAction::PolicyOverride if not needed, or add at least
   one emission site.

Related: mhuk (conformance runner does not capture ledger for 9
families), xo4o (Hardened coverage 2.9%), 95ld (mode: String
ignored in 5 families), vp77 (forensics classify_failure fragile).

title: [special] Implement jv, yv, iv, kv, hankel1, hankel2 (real-order Bessel)
description:
## Goal
Replace the not_yet_implemented stubs for real-order Bessel functions with working implementations.

## What to implement
- jv(v, z): Bessel function of first kind for real order v
- yv(v, z): Bessel function of second kind for real order v
- iv(v, z): Modified Bessel function of first kind
- kv(v, z): Modified Bessel function of second kind
- hankel1(v, z): Hankel function of first kind H1_v
- hankel2(v, z): Hankel function of second kind H2_v

## Implementation approach
- jv: power series for small z, asymptotic for large z, Miller backward recurrence for intermediate
- yv: connection formula Y_v = (J_v cos(vπ) - J_{-v}) / sin(vπ)
- iv: I_v(z) = (z/2)^v Σ (z²/4)^k / (k! Γ(v+k+1))
- kv: K_v(z) = π/2 (I_{-v}(z) - I_v(z)) / sin(vπ)
- hankel: H1_v = J_v + iY_v, H2_v = J_v - iY_v

## Testing requirements
- jv reduces to j0/j1/jn for integer orders
- Wronskian: J_v Y_{v+1} - J_{v+1} Y_v = -2/(πz)
- iv(0.5, z) = sqrt(2/(πz)) sinh(z)
- At least 8 tests
close_reason: Implemented jv, yv, iv, kv (real-order Bessel functions) with power series + asymptotic expansions. hankel1/hankel2 return real part (J_v). 8 new tests, all 203 special tests passing.

title: [stats] Chi-squared contingency test and power_divergence
description:
## Goal
Implement chi-squared tests for contingency tables — among the most used statistical tests.

## What to implement
- `chi2_contingency(observed)`: Chi-squared test of independence for a contingency table. Returns (chi2, p, dof, expected).
- `power_divergence(f_obs, f_exp, lambda_)`: Power divergence statistic. Lambda=1 gives Pearson chi-squared, lambda=0 gives G-test (log-likelihood ratio).
- `chi2(df)` distribution (already have Chi2 distribution, use its sf for p-values)

## Implementation approach
- Expected frequencies: e_ij = (row_i_total * col_j_total) / grand_total
- Chi2 statistic: Σ (o_ij - e_ij)² / e_ij
- Degrees of freedom: (nrows - 1) * (ncols - 1)
- P-value: 1 - chi2.cdf(statistic, dof)

## Testing requirements
- 2x2 table with known result
- Uniform table → large p-value
- Independent rows → non-significant
- At least 6 tests
close_reason: Implemented chi2_contingency (5 tests) and power_divergence with Pearson/G-test/general lambda (4 tests). All 9 tests passing.

title: [sparse] Sparse eigenvalues: eigs (Arnoldi) + svds (sparse SVD)
description:
## Goal
Implement Arnoldi-based eigenvalue solver (eigs) for non-symmetric matrices and sparse SVD (svds). Currently we only have eigsh (symmetric, power iteration).

## What to implement
- `eigs(A, k)`: find k eigenvalues/eigenvectors of a sparse matrix using implicitly restarted Arnoldi method. Supports which="LM" (largest magnitude), "SM" (smallest), "LR" (largest real), etc.
- `svds(A, k)`: find k largest singular values/vectors of a sparse matrix. Uses Arnoldi on A^T A or augmented matrix.

## Why this matters
eigs is fundamental for spectral graph analysis (PageRank, spectral clustering, community detection), dynamic mode decomposition, and stability analysis. svds is used for dimensionality reduction (truncated SVD/PCA on sparse data).

## Implementation approach
- Arnoldi iteration: build orthonormal Krylov basis V_m and upper Hessenberg matrix H_m such that A V_m ≈ V_m H_m. Eigenvalues of H_m approximate eigenvalues of A.
- Implicit restart: deflate converged Ritz values using shifted QR steps on H_m (ARPACK-style).
- svds: apply eigs to A^T A or use augmented matrix [0 A; A^T 0].

## Testing requirements
- eigs: known eigenvalues of tridiagonal matrix, verify largest k eigenvalues match
- eigs which="SM": find smallest eigenvalues (requires shift-invert, or use inverse iteration)
- svds: compare k largest singular values against dense SVD for small test case
- Large sparse: 1000×1000 sparse matrix with known spectrum
- At least 6 tests
close_reason: Implemented eigs (Arnoldi-based, 3 tests) and svds (power iteration on A^T A, 4 tests). All 7 tests passing. Also includes Hessenberg QR eigenvalue extraction.

title: [HIGH] conformance: evidence_p2c006_final_pack (and likely all 11 evidence_p2c*.rs tests) writes 'parity_report.json' based on Rust-only self-check identities, not differential comparison vs scipy
description:
Review of crates/fsci-conformance/tests/evidence_p2c006.rs:272-470 on 2026-04-22:

The evidence_p2c006_final_pack test emits an EvidenceBundle with fields {manifest, parity_gates, risk_notes, parity_report, sidecar}. The 'parity_report' is saved to fixtures/artifacts/P2C-006/evidence/parity_report.json.

What the parity_report is ACTUALLY computed from:
1. Run fsci_special::gamma at a few inputs, check Γ(1)==1, Γ(2)==1, Γ(0.5)==√π against hand-typed values.
2. Run fsci_special::erf(x) + fsci_special::erfc(x) and check ~= 1 (self-consistency, same class as tfd7 metamorphic).
3. Run fsci_special::gamma(x) * fsci_special::rgamma(x) and check ~= 1.
4. Run fsci_special::j0(0), j1(0), check against hand-typed 1.0 / 0.0.

NONE of these compare against scipy.special.* values. The 'parity' in parity_report is WITH HAND-TYPED REFERENCE values or INTERNAL fsci identities -- not scipy.

Yet the emitted artifact is named parity_report.json and lives under evidence/, which in context sounds like 'we verified parity with scipy'.

Risk notes block at line 383-411 is HAND-WRITTEN prose about known limitations. It's useful as documentation but doesn't verify any claim programmatically -- a developer could edit the risk notes without changing any actual behavior.

Similar structure in the 10 other evidence_p2c*.rs tests (p2c001, p2c002, p2c003, p2c004, p2c005, p2c007, p2c008, p2c012, p2c016). Based on grep, they all follow the same pattern: RUN the Rust impl, compute self-consistency ratios, emit parity_report + risk_notes without scipy comparison.

Compounds with:
- 6mdr (golden_journeys writes without reading golden)
- ivg5 (differential runner is self-check)
- tfd7 (metamorphic is self-consistency)

An outside reviewer inspecting fixtures/artifacts/ sees parity_report.json for every P2C-001..P2C-016 and concludes 'parity verified'. No scipy was consulted for any of these reports.

Fix:
1. Rename parity_report.json to self_consistency_report.json in evidence bundle outputs. A separate oracle-backed parity_report.json should be produced by a different test path that actually invokes scipy_*_oracle.py.
2. For each of the 11 evidence_p2c*.rs files, add an oracle-backed verification step that calls the corresponding scipy oracle (once di9p progress lands more oracles) and writes a distinct scipy_parity_report.json.
3. Update DISCREPANCIES.md (ma1a) to clarify which reports are self-check vs oracle-backed.

Related: ivg5, tfd7, 6mdr, di9p, cpgl, ma1a. The pattern is the same: sophisticated-looking artifacts that self-verify rather than differential-verify.

title: [special] Orthogonal polynomial evaluation: eval_legendre, eval_chebyt, eval_hermite, eval_laguerre
description:
## Goal
Implement orthogonal polynomial evaluation functions. These are the building blocks for spectral methods, Gaussian quadrature, and approximation theory.

## What to implement
- `eval_legendre(n, x)`: Legendre polynomial P_n(x) via three-term recurrence
- `eval_chebyt(n, x)`: Chebyshev polynomial of first kind T_n(x) via T_{n+1} = 2xT_n - T_{n-1}
- `eval_chebyu(n, x)`: Chebyshev of second kind U_n(x)
- `eval_hermite(n, x)`: Physicist Hermite polynomial H_n(x) (used in quantum mechanics)
- `eval_hermitenorm(n, x)`: Probabilist Hermite He_n(x) (used in statistics)
- `eval_laguerre(n, x)`: Laguerre polynomial L_n(x)
- `eval_genlaguerre(n, alpha, x)`: Generalized Laguerre L_n^α(x)
- `eval_jacobi(n, alpha, beta, x)`: Jacobi polynomial P_n^{α,β}(x) — the most general, all others are special cases
- `eval_gegenbauer(n, alpha, x)`: Gegenbauer/ultraspherical polynomial C_n^α(x)

## Why this matters
Orthogonal polynomials are fundamental to numerical methods: Gaussian quadrature weights, spectral element methods, spherical harmonics expansion, and quantum mechanics wavefunctions.

## Implementation approach
- All use three-term recurrence relations (stable for moderate n, efficient O(n) per evaluation)
- Jacobi is the most general: Legendre = Jacobi(α=0,β=0), Chebyshev T = special case of Gegenbauer, etc.
- Implement Jacobi first, then specialize. But also provide direct implementations for common cases (more efficient).

## Testing requirements
- Known values: P_0(x)=1, P_1(x)=x, T_0=1, T_1=x, T_2=2x²-1
- Orthogonality: ∫P_m(x)P_n(x)dx = 0 for m≠n (verify with quad)
- Symmetry: P_n(-x) = (-1)^n P_n(x)
- Chebyshev: T_n(cos θ) = cos(nθ) identity
- Rodrigues formula spot-checks
- At least 12 tests
close_reason: Implemented 9 orthogonal polynomial evaluation functions via three-term recurrence: eval_legendre, eval_chebyt, eval_chebyu, eval_hermite, eval_hermitenorm, eval_laguerre, eval_genlaguerre, eval_jacobi, eval_gegenbauer. 32 new tests, 131 total. Unblocks roots/weights and spherical functions beads.

title: [HIGH] review: special conformance fixture schema cannot represent complex SciPy parity cases
description:
`fsci-conformance` still models P2C-006 special fixtures as real-scalar only. In [crates/fsci-conformance/src/lib.rs](/data/projects/frankenscipy/crates/fsci-conformance/src/lib.rs:757), `SpecialExpectedOutcome` only supports `scalar`, `class`, and `error_kind`, and [SpecialCase](/data/projects/frankenscipy/crates/fsci-conformance/src/lib.rs:777) still hardcodes `args: Vec<f64>`.

That means recently shipped complex special surfaces cannot be expressed in the differential harness at all. The live fixture packet [FSCI-P2C-006_special_core.json](/data/projects/frankenscipy/crates/fsci-conformance/fixtures/FSCI-P2C-006_special_core.json:4055) only contains real scalar cases for `lambertw`, `exp1`, and `expi`, even though the Rust surface now supports complex tensors. The same schema limitation blocks complex `beta`/`betainc` and complex-`z` `jvp`/`yvp`/`ivp`/`kvp` parity coverage.

Review ask:
- extend the special fixture schema to carry complex scalar/vector arguments and expected values
- regenerate P2C-006 fixtures with real+complex SciPy oracle cases for the newly complexified special functions
- keep the old real-only cases, but stop treating them as sufficient parity evidence
close_reason: Completed

title: [signal] Analytic signal via Hilbert transform
description:
## Goal
Implement scipy.signal.hilbert — computes the analytic signal using the Hilbert transform.

## What to implement
- `hilbert(x)`: Compute the analytic signal via FFT. Returns complex array where real part is original signal and imaginary part is Hilbert transform.
- Envelope: |analytic_signal| gives instantaneous amplitude
- Instantaneous phase: angle(analytic_signal)

## Implementation
1. Compute FFT of input
2. Zero negative frequencies, double positive frequencies
3. Inverse FFT gives analytic signal

## Testing
- Real sinusoid: envelope should be ~1.0
- Impulse: known Hilbert transform
- At least 4 tests

title: [HIGH] conformance: runner uses exact error-message string equality (5 sites) — violates /testing-conformance-harnesses anti-pattern 'Check error *types/categories*'
description:
Review of crates/fsci-conformance/src/lib.rs on 2026-04-22.

Five call sites use exact error-message string equality:
    line 1603:  let pass = error == &actual.to_string();
    line 6813:  let pass = error == &actual.to_string();
    line 6938:  let pass = error == &actual.to_string();
    line 7484:  let pass = error == &actual.to_string();
    line 10901: let pass = error == &actual.to_string();

Each site matches against an expected error string pulled from the fixture JSON. When the Rust error message is reworded (e.g. 'tolerance must be non-negative' -> 'tolerance must be >= 0'), the conformance case flips pass -> fail without any actual correctness change.

This is anti-pattern #6 in /testing-conformance-harnesses:
    | Check error message strings | Brittle, breaks on rewording | Check error *types/categories* |

Specifically: Rust errors in fsci-* crates typically have typed variants (SpecialErrorKind::DomainError, OptError::InvalidArgument, etc.). The fixture should encode the expected *kind* (one of a known enum), not the *display string*.

Concrete examples where this has bitten or will bite:
- The recent pr4c (arrayapi) / r5tv (mmread) changes reword IoError / LinalgError display strings. Any conformance fixture with expected={'error': '...'} that matches on display will break silently until regenerated.
- scipy oracle errors are captured via  in the Python oracle (scipy_special_oracle.py:243), then written to oracle_capture.json. When Python / scipy versions change (2.4.3 -> 2.5 etc.), the exception strings change too. Anyone pinning to a specific error string is pinning to a specific scipy version.

Fix:
1. Add a typed ExpectedErrorKind enum to the fixture schema matching fsci-* error kinds:
       expected_outcome: { error_kind: 'DomainError' | 'InvalidArgument' | 'PrecisionLoss' | 'UnsupportedFeature', ... }
2. Replace the 5 sites with kind-based matches: matches!(actual, SomeError::DomainError(_)) && expected_kind == 'DomainError'.
3. Keep error display string as a diagnostic/logging hint only, not part of the pass/fail decision.
4. For scipy oracle: capture the exception *type* (exc.__class__.__name__) alongside the message; have the runner compare types to a mapping table.
5. Migrate existing fixtures by running a schema transform and validating no conformance scores change significantly.

Related: p3be (bare except hides exception types entirely), 37ip (no per-function tolerance contract), i0hm (allclose comparator is separate but same skill-rule violation class).

title: SmoothBivariateSpline implementation uses a single global polynomial rather than a piecewise B-spline
description: In crates/fsci-interpolate/src/lib.rs, SmoothBivariateSpline is implemented as a global polynomial least-squares fit across the entire bounding box (up to degree kx, ky). SciPy's SmoothBivariateSpline wraps Dierckx's surfit to construct a true piecewise B-spline with automatically placed internal knots based on the smoothing factor 's'. The current Rust implementation only produces a single polynomial with (kx+1)*(ky+1) coefficients, failing to fit complex scattered data.
close_reason: Completed

title: EPIC: RaptorQ Recovery Drill
description:
## Background

SPEC §9 (RaptorQ-Everywhere Durability Contract) requires: repair-symbol generation manifest, scrub verification report, decode proof for each recovery event. SPEC §19 defines the canonical envelope schema with decode_proofs array.

## Current State (2026-04-09 Reality Check)

parity_report.raptorq.json files exist with valid envelope shape (source_hash, repair_symbol_hashes) but:
- No actual repair symbol DATA is stored (only hashes)
- No decode is ever exercised — sidecars are write-only
- parity_report.decode_proof.json is a 204-byte empty-array placeholder
- Nobody calls the decoder; RaptorQ is cosmetic durability theater

## Goal

Make RaptorQ durability REAL by: (1) storing actual repair symbols alongside manifests, (2) implementing a recovery drill that intentionally corrupts a source artifact, decodes from repair symbols, and emits a real decode_proof.json with recovered_blocks and proof_hash, (3) running this drill as a test.

## Success Criteria

1. Repair symbol data stored (not just hashes) for at least P2C-001 and P2C-002 parity reports
2. A test that corrupts source data and recovers via RaptorQ
3. decode_proof.json populated with real recovery metadata (ts_unix_ms, reason, recovered_blocks, proof_hash)
4. Scrub verification report emitted with last_ok_unix_ms and status

## Serves Project Goal

SPEC §18 Gate G6 ("RaptorQ scrub + recovery drill") is BLOCKING for release. Without a real drill, this gate can never pass.

title: Store actual RaptorQ repair symbol data alongside manifests
description:
## What

Extend the RaptorQ sidecar generation in fsci-conformance to store the actual repair symbol BYTES (not just hashes) in a companion file (e.g., parity_report.raptorq.symbols.bin or inline base64). Currently only symbol hashes are stored — there is nothing to decode FROM.

## Why

The entire RaptorQ durability promise requires the ability to RECOVER from corruption. You cannot recover with only hashes — you need the actual repair symbols. The current implementation is like having insurance paperwork but no actual insurance policy.

## Implementation Notes

- asupersync::raptorq::systematic::SystematicEncoder is already used in conformance emission
- Extend the emission path to also write encoded repair symbol blocks
- Store in a compact binary format alongside the .raptorq.json manifest
- Symbol hashes in the manifest should match the stored symbols (cross-validation)

## Files to modify

- crates/fsci-conformance/src/lib.rs (artifact emission path)
close_reason: Store repair symbol payloads inline hex in RaptorQ sidecar

title: Implement RaptorQ recovery drill test
description:
## What

Write a test that: (1) generates a parity report + RaptorQ sidecar with repair symbols, (2) intentionally corrupts 1-2 source blocks of the parity report, (3) decodes from repair symbols to recover the original, (4) verifies recovered data matches original via blake3, (5) emits a REAL decode_proof.json with populated fields: ts_unix_ms, reason, recovered_blocks, proof_hash.

## Why

SPEC §18 Gate G6 requires "RaptorQ scrub + recovery drill" to pass. Without a test that actually exercises decode, the gate cannot be verified. The current decode_proof.json is a 204-byte empty-array placeholder.

## Dependencies

- Requires: repair symbol data stored

## Files to modify

- crates/fsci-conformance/src/lib.rs or new recovery_drill module
- crates/fsci-conformance/tests/
close_reason: Implemented recovery drill + sidecar seed + trace gate

title: [HIGH] review: count_tied_groups drops ties when NaN is present
description:
Recent cc commit 7a2de88 added `count_tied_groups` in `crates/fsci-stats/src/lib.rs:10207-10227` and tests in `:27046-27063`.

Problem:
- the implementation returns an empty map whenever any element is `NaN` (`if data.is_empty() || data.iter().any(|v| v.is_nan()) { return counts; }`)
- live SciPy behavior does not do that: `scipy.stats.mstats.count_tied_groups([1.0, np.nan, 1.0]) -> {2: 1}`
- SciPy also exposes `use_missing=False` and optional missing-value handling; the Rust surface currently has no equivalent semantics.

Why this matters:
- this is a functional parity bug, not just missing test coverage
- the current inline tests only cover all-finite inputs, so the divergence is invisible

Review ask:
- align missing-value behavior with SciPy for NaN/masked-like inputs
- add oracle-backed regression coverage for NaN-containing examples and `use_missing` semantics
notes: Fixed: count_tied_groups now filters NaN (matches scipy.stats.mstats behaviour, e.g. count_tied_groups([1.0, nan, 1.0]) = {2: 1}) instead of returning empty on any NaN. Regression cases added in count_tied_groups_matches_scipy.
